title: Making a clamshell box
subtitle: A double-tray enclosure that protects a finished book at head, tail, and fore-edge
excerpt: A clamshell box is two shallow trays hinged at the spine edge, one fitting snugly inside the other. It is the archival standard for protecting a finished bound book or a collection of loose sheets. Making one is a board-cutting and covering exercise that uses the same skills as case binding.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on boxes and slipcases. Internet Archive scan available. The specific clamshell design described here follows the basic double-tray construction consistent with Cockerell's and later conservation literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: An enclosure for a book made from two shallow trays hinged together, one slightly smaller than the other so it nests inside when closed.
One half of a clamshell box: a flat board base with walls on three sides, open at the fourth side.
The wall of the outer tray that forms the hinge joint with the inner tray, running the full height of the box.

content.text: A
clamshell box

protects a book at all six surfaces. It holds the book snugly and absorbs impact at the head, tail, and fore-edge. Accurate measuring is everything. The inner tray must fit inside the outer tray without forcing or rattling.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Use a fresh blade for the long straight cuts through 3 mm board. Cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand well back from the blade path.
What you need
Measure the book to be boxed: height (H), width (W), and thickness (T). Note measurements to the nearest half millimetre.
Board: 3 mm bookbinding board for the outer tray base and walls; 2 mm board for the inner tray base and walls. The wall height of each tray is T/2 plus 3 mm to allow for a finger lift.
Covering: book cloth for the exterior, endpaper stock or washi for the tray interiors.
Cutting the boards
Outer tray base: H + 3 mm by W + 3 mm (the extra allows for the board thickness of the inner tray walls).
Inner tray base: H by W exactly.
Tray walls: cut the four wall pieces for each tray. The
spine wall
runs the full height of the base; the head, tail, and fore-edge walls butt up against it and against each other.
Assembling the trays
Apply PVA to the inner face of each wall piece. Glue each wall to the base, checking for square with a combination square. The walls stand upright at 90 degrees to the base.
Clamp the walls in place with binder clips while the PVA sets, at least 20 minutes. Build both trays this way.
Check the fit: the inner
tray
should slide into the outer tray with a snug but not forced fit. Sand inner tray walls lightly if too tight.
Covering and hinging

Cover the exterior of each tray with book cloth, mitring the corners neatly. Line the interior walls and base with endpaper stock or washi. Hinge the two trays together by gluing a strip of book cloth across the outer spine wall of both trays, allowing a gap of 3 mm at the hinge joint for the cloth to flex. Press flat overnight.
Finishing

Add a ribbon marker inside the inner tray: a 300 mm length of satin ribbon glued under the base lining lets the book be lifted out without gripping the covers. Label the spine of the outer tray in pencil inside the cloth flap.
Care

Store boxes upright on a shelf with similar-height boxes to support them laterally. Avoid stacking boxes more than three deep; the bottom box takes all the weight. Re-cover a worn box with fresh book cloth when the spine wall cloth frays at the hinge.
